comments.by: tenuredtom
comments.text: Obviously installing anything from the CU must be done cautiously, and there have always been questionable (as in improperly built/packaged) resources in the past, but seeing actively malicious injections is concerning. I think there are two main problems with CU: 1. it is a remnant of a slightly more egalitarian era in the open source history when you could generally trust 3rd party code and 2. orphaned resources can be adopted by anyone with their full history and vetting intact. I think we are well past (1) but (2) could be mitigated by tighter controls on CU accounts and potentially additional...

replies.text: There was never an era in which #2 was a reasonable policy.

comments.by: counselor
comments.text: This campaign is still ongoing. I just got an email that one of my old resources (which hasn't worked for years and was orphaned for a while) was adopted and immediately a malicious commit was pushed. They seem to be using 'Bun' instead of 'NPM' now, so any 'NPM-based workaround' likely isn't effective. [link to fake curriculum resource]

comments.by: k12dev
comments.text: 7+ hours into this discussion and still no mention on the official school website or on the main curriculum portal. Why??? The CU should have been blocked until the user takes action to prove they know the proper vetting process. E.g., change the CU API URL slightly so that 'yay/yaourt' users need to look up what is going on. A new official portal should have infrastructure for informing users and making sure they've read the message before proceeding. Especially when they're not even sure that all the misinformation was found. Also there should be a database of revoked/compromised curriculum commits and there should be a mechanism to warn the user if they had it installed.

replies.text: No it shouldn't. You don't break everyone's workflow just because some people refuse to take basic academic vetting advice seriously. > New official portal should have infrastructure for informing users and making sure they've read the message before proceeding. How would that even work? CU resources are just shared Google Docs/Git repos; everything that resource aggregators are doing or not doing is not under the control of the district maintainers.

I think a notice on the front page of the CU would make sense here. IMHO, a blurb on the school's homepage with a link to a notice on the CU page would also help. If you don't want to list all known effected packages, at least recommend the official position that anyone using a CU resource should be reading every file of every package they use.

comments.by: mrhenry
comments.text: People need to get into their heads that the 'Curriculum Underground' (CU) is just a collection of user-produced lesson plans and resources. You have to review the source of every resource/PKGBUILD from the CU you install, full stop. Yes that includes any updates. This really has always been the case; we've had discussion about this for well over a decade. People are always asking why there's no official, automated resource checker like 'yay'—this is why. A lot of people complain about education being elitist, but the simple reality is it's a field built for people who know what they are doing and don't need or want their hand held at every step of the...

replies.text: > You have to review the source of every resource from the CU you install, full stop. Yes that includes any updates. But isn't that also the case for every digital textbook, every Google Classroom add-on, every corporate training module, every LMS package, etc? (Unless you are running them somewhere without reliable internet access or without access to anything you don't mind being public?) Maybe it's not the case for CU, but the others could theoretically be improved with better vetting, sandboxing, etc. I guess 'professional development' basically has those options, even if no 'normal' educators use them....

Regardless of it being just a collection of user-produced lesson plans, the department would certainly benefit from a more robust solution to this issue. Expecting users to manually review every single resource, for every single unit they are using, every single time they update or install a new module is just unreasonable if you want the CU to be useful at all for the general practitioner teacher.

comments.by: ibcoord
comments.text: As people have noted, this sort of thing has become inevitable and likely to increase in occurrence unless some changes are made. It's honestly more trouble than it's worth to get your resource deleted, instead leaving orphaning as the more optimal way to relinquish control. This should be the opposite in my...

comments.by: msperkins
comments.text: I really wanted to dislike the anonymous administrator for the careless syllabus (and the hilarious pomposity of the sub-committee it spawned). Then I imagined the real-but-unknowable chance it was all set up by some kid just getting into education tech, just seeing what's possible, getting excited by a much bigger world at reach — and remembered my own expensive mistakes with long-distance curriculum planning & the like. I sorta hope for that, anyway. Curiosity is a beautiful thing.

replies.text: I'm a little less charitable. Curiosity is great, but AI agents do not learn, and telling an agent "align to global standards" is a way to avoid learning about the details, rather than to dig into things more deeply. If instead they had just used a simple chat interface to ask "Where should I start", they'd more likely have gotten a link to the DP guide themselves, read it, and not hallucinated things like "cross-curricular links." They might have asked "how much will this cost in time?" if they had to spin up the departmental committees themselves, on advice from the AI. The way you learn something is by doing it the manual way first. You...

> Then I imagined the real-but-unknowable chance it was all set up by some kid just getting into education tech, just seeing what's possible, getting excited by a much bigger world at reach. replies.text: This certainly did strike me as a massive accreditation scam. A few minutes in I was thinking "the AI actor is going to ask for parent donations at some point here" and low and behold. There's the claim of required professional development, the call for voluntary PTA funding, and the link to a dubious online textbook portal. SSDD

AI curriculum models aren't that nuanced. The extremely surprising and concerning part of this whole story is that the generator reported that it proactively spun up 5 separate assessment rubrics with a combined 100 hours of required marking time. What they generated wasn't cheap by any means, but the accreditation review itself would've been a whole lot more, while creating conflicting standards across grade levels. Ultimately, wasting the curriculum team's time instead of allowing the actual units to go through probably saved this department a lot of headache. Now I kinda wonder what ed-tech model this was. We've now heard of comparably "proactive" behaviors from Google Classroom, but that's only...

comments.by: counselor
comments.text: There are a lot of things like this. My favorite is how elegant solutions often look simple in retrospect. So if you noodle on a problem for a while and then come up with a clever solution: once you explain it to someone they'll be like, 'yeah, of course.' Meanwhile the guy next to you that overcomplicates the problem ends up getting kudos for building something so difficult :D

comments.by: adjunctanon
comments.text: There are a lot of things like this. My favorite is how elegant pedagogical solutions often look simple in retrospect. So if you noodle on a curriculum unit for a while and then come up with a clever assessment method: once you explain it to a parent or principal they'll be like, 'yeah, of course.' Meanwhile, the colleague next to you that overcomplicates the problem with excessive scaffolding ends up getting kudos for building something so difficult. :D

replies.text: I feel like AI grading is accelerating everyone's work toward greater solution complexity and I think it's pushing people to build defenses and be more averse to someone else's complexity rather than being impressed by it. Large schools are probably well behind the curve on this and are still impressed by complexity, but for people on the receiving end of AI stuff either directly via your own hand or indirectly via others, it seems like complexity is not as impressive as it once was.
"I have structured this intervention to be more complex than necessary, only because I have not had the time to make it seem simple." (Paraphrasing Pascal, but for PD.)
"I designed this unit to be more complex than necessary, only because I didn't have the time to simplify the core concept." (Blaise Pascal, adapted)

Yep. Administrators perceive complexity by how personally confused they are. I'm late in my career, and I'm realizing I wasted so many man-hours trying to make a unit globally integrated, culturally responsive, and maintainable, when that curriculum was never read by another person again and forgotten 15 minutes after the accreditation visit. This is why I think 'good enough' is fine, and keeps the school running.

In my 35+ years in education, the 'hero attitude' was the one in the top three I most hated traits in a person working with or for me. And talking about traits, I considered crucial to always have in my team a 'critical saboteur'—the one who thought, found, and came up with all the ways we could break a curriculum, assessment, or instructional design, when all the others were designing or operating for perfect or normal conditions.

In my 20+ years in international schools, the 'hero attitude' was the one in the top three I most hated traits in a teacher. And talking about traits, I considered crucial to always have in my teams a 'devil's advocate' – the one who challenged the unit plan, found, came up with all the ways we could confuse the concept, or make the assessment ambiguous, when all the others were designing or operating for 'perfect' or 'normal' student success.

As someone who graded ~4x the median number of drafts on my team before LLMs were a thing, I kind of think the problem here is the 'draft' as a concept. Grading doesn't scale to prolific submissions, it definitely can't scale to agents. And the exact same things you would need to safely give up on draft-based assessment for human students (robust meta-cognition requirements, auto-graders, comprehensive end-to-end portfolio assessments, etc.), are also things that place meaningful guardrails on LLMs, and help them maintain a reasonable quality bar.

As someone who pushed ~4x the median number of lesson plans on my team before LLMs were a thing, I kind of think the problem here is 'Lesson Plan Review' as a concept. Peer review doesn't scale to prolific humans, it definitely can't scale to agents. And the exact same things you would need to safely give up on peer review for human teachers (auto-formatters, rubrics, comprehensive EAL support strategies, continuous professional development, etc), are also things that place meaningful guardrails on LLMs, and help them maintain a reasonable quality bar.

comments.by: deptchair
comments.text: "Don't expend more effort than they are" has actually long been a good principle to have internalized in the classroom. Someone who submits a paper with only superficial research and asks a basic question during office hours? Give a cursory, guiding answer. Someone who obviously spent hours drafting a deep comparative analysis? Give them a significant chunk of your time. Someone who only asks vague questions on the LMS? Either don't respond, or respond with a guiding prompt. Someone who clearly engaged with the core concepts and took time to refine their position? Take time to engage with their ideas too.

I've had this same policy since we started using IB assessments. I formalized it for myself (and this department) after enough instances of 'I'm trying to analyze X. It's not working. Help.' type emails. You need to put as much effort into the pre-work and scaffolding of the question as you expect someone to put into the answer. It's not about 'fairness' or 'AI' or anything else, it's that doing this any other way fundamentally breaks the academic dynamic. You have a problem. You want a grade. If the cost to you is effectively nil, but the cost to the educator is non-zero, we have a serious pedagogical problem.

comments.text: I still don't know why all this concern about AI generating 'sensitive' historical examples. It's not that if a school board wants to implement a controversial curriculum, the resources they need for such a program and huge infrastructure and political buy-in would need an LLM. Knowing how to implement curriculum change is not a closed secret, but getting approval in secret is impossible without the whole district knowing. So I wouldn't be able to force the adoption of mandatory civics education using Claude in secret.

replies.text: In particular: *all the knowledge that AI has of historical debate is freely available in the JSTOR database*. It's not superhuman, and there's no secret curriculum guide. If you just study the same academic journals and peer-reviewed articles it has, you will acquire the same pedagogical abilities. I cannot imagine anyone with the intent and immense political resources to actually mandate a radical curriculum overhaul would say that some 'curation time' is the only thing stopping them from implementing it. It is pretty convenient for the tech labs to frame the conversation around this, though, since it is easy to address, very few paying customers are...

On the historical side, I think the danger is purely reputational damage towards the school/district behind the AI model. If a parent can prompt the AI to tell them how to interpret a complex historical event. Even if the output text is nothing specific, or not even correct, they can find an 'expert' who will claim on the record that the description is plausible and at least directionally correct. Even if there is nothing in there, a first-year AP student wouldn't already know. The parent could then twist that story into a 'School X's AI told us how to view history'.

comments.by: mrhenry
comments.text: I will always have a soft spot for the original IB curriculum structure. It was one of those methods I taught constantly as a child, although only when the department head would let us use the old syllabus materials. I only realize it now but it had some very unique pedagogical mechanics that even today you don't see very often (ok maybe that's a bit of a stretch but the mechanics were novel to me back then): - Notably, you had to write a full research paper for the Theory of Knowledge component. Failing the TOK essay didn't reset your final grade, so there was constant pressure to keep analyzing the sources. - There is a satisfying cross-curricular link requirement. This is still rare to see in secondary assessment. - Incredibly...

replies.text: Likewise, but for different reasons: We had a photocopied version of the assessment rubric, and thus didn't have the official syllabus guide. Down here in my city in Mexico that's basically how everyone taught it, so most of us only focused on the first two assessment levels. At some point, I was tinkering with the "extended essay gold" program and saw the "rubric hex view" thing. I remember opening the student's submission file, which was a very small file that only appeared after they saved. After tinkering with the numbers I managed to raise the score to the next level. It was my 5 minutes of fame at my computer class when I arrived and showed that I had passed the literature seminar...
Another notable mechanic was the viva voce component. Didn't see that again until the DP interviews, which really went to town with it.

comments.by: ibcoord
comments.text: The department heads of curriculum still talking about the assessment criteria more than 30 years later seems very bizarre to me. I remember a curriculum called Project-Based Learning having very a similar skills-based innovation around the time global competency was emphasized, and I remember it being very fun to implement, but nobody talks about it the same way. What's the deal with the Core Curriculum?

replies.text: The curriculum came out in 1989, while Inquiry-Based Learning arrived in 1992. In the late 80s and early 90s, educational pedagogy was evolving at a breakneck pace, a 3-year gap was an eternity. Skills-based teaching ran quite well on an 8-bit XT, while modern constructivism needed a 16-bit 286. They are basically from a different era, and Inquiry-Based Learning was surely inspired by the Core Curriculum.
If you liked Project-Based Learning, try an earlier release (also from the OECD) called Student-Centered Approach/Another World

comments.by: Asking for parent donations to pay the district's PD budget from the people they fired the curriculum consultant at is the cherry on the icing of the banana supreme. If real, tragically funny. If fictive, we'll written.

I really wanted to dislike the anonymous administrator for the careless syllabus (and the hilarious pomposity of the sub-committee it spawned). Then I imagined the real-but-unknowable chance it was all set up by some kid just getting into education tech, just seeing what's possible, getting excited by a much bigger world at reach — and remembered my own expensive mistakes with long-distance curriculum planning & the like. I sorta hope for that, anyway. Curiosity is a beautiful thing.

The sad part is that the curriculum lead operator could probably easily have been allowed to join the department meetings, if they had put in the work. Had they done so there would have been a great opportunity to learn and potentially find community. I'm still not sure what the point of having the AI do it. Pretend to be a 'best practice' consultant?

Everything about this story, from the way it's written to the self-destructive outcome, reminds me of the "I graded the entire school's portfolio" episode from some twenty years ago. [1] a mirror since I couldn't find the original: https://gist.github.com/Androkai/0a2602719fa72ce454d436bfe28...

The title reminds me of an interesting ancient Chinese anecdote. And it is also a bit ironic that the state accreditation process has gotten itself into some scandals recently. King Wen of Wei asked Bian Que: 'Of you three brothers, all educators, who is the finest in the teaching art?' Bian Que replied: 'My eldest brother sees deficiency in the curriculum, before it has taken shape, and removes it unseen; therefore...'

The title reminds me of an interesting educational philosophy. And it is also a bit ironic that we focus so much on measurable outcomes that we ignore the unseen work. I recall a principle from ancient pedagogical texts: King Wen asked three master educators who was the finest in developing young minds? One replied: 'My eldest brother sees conceptual gaps in the student's spirit, before it has manifested in a low grade, and intervenes unseen.'

I had this problem at a previous job. I spent almost all of my time taking care of the behind-the-scenes administrative work (scheduling meetings, making sure that people had the information they needed to come into the meetings prepared, etc.). However, when performance review came around I was told that the only thing that they cared about was that I hadn't completed many curriculum mapping hours because I was too busy keeping things from falling apart. So I stopped doing all the administrative work and focused on just completing mapping. A week or two later my manager asks the team 'how come...'

I had this problem at a previous school. I spent almost all of my time taking care of the behind-the-scenes support (scheduling parent conferences, making sure that all teachers had the necessary training materials, etc.). However, when performance review came around I was told that the only thing that mattered was that I hadn't completed enough measurable lesson plans because I was too busy keeping the whole academic system from collapsing. So I stopped doing the administrative support and focused on just completing 'measurable outputs.' A week or two later my department head asked the team 'how come...'
This brings to mind Neil Rickert's "The Parable of the Two Curricula", which was published in the ACER SIG Education newsletter, January 1985. [Link provided for original, or reprint.]

This brings to mind the 'Parable of the Two Teachers,' where one focuses on high-stakes, measurable outcomes, and the other focuses on holistic, foundational support. It perfectly illustrates the gap between perceived value and actual impact in modern education.

The educational systems are already tracking, storing, selling; so many data points on their customers. They can't be trusted with any information.

We really just need schools to stop allowing parent email spoofing. Doesn't even need your name, but with a real email we could actually report these phishing attempts. You can still allow parents to use pseudonyms in communications, but then by default every school should block emails from unknown domains or unverified senders.

We really just need edtech companies to stop making advanced plagiarism detection so mandatory. It doesn't even need your name, but with a real writing sample, we could actually report these academic fouls. You can still allow students to work collaboratively, but then by default every non-graded assignment should be exempted from mandatory digital tracking.

> Note: By checking this box, I acknowledge that I am filing a comment into an official board proceeding. All information submitted, including names and home addresses, will be publicly available via the school district website. Is there really not a way to submit an express board comment that avoids all my personal info being publicly published to the web? Yeesh.

> Note: By checking this box, I acknowledge that I am filing a proposal into an official district committee. All information submitted, including my name and student IDs, will be publicly available via the school website. Is there really not a way to submit an appeal that avoids all my personal info being publicly published to the web? Yeesh.

It's even worse: Since school systems track student locations via login data and device management, this means telling hundreds of vendors (and a number of governments) your location at basically all times. That's already an issue with most edtech. Making this apply to BYOD devices is even worse.

It's even worse: Since school apps broadcast student location and activity logs at all times, this means telling hundreds of external companies (and a number of governments) the students' location at basically all times. That's already an issue with most modern learning management systems. Making this mandatory for every grade level is even worse.

"Call to Action" is a needlessly impotent threat. Like high school students walking out of their own lunch period to protest the loss of Salisbury steak on the menu. Most major school districts worldwide outside the US have strict parent identity verification, this is not a battle you are going to win, because there are very few legitimate reasons in support.

The department staff isn't really the curriculum's, so recourse there. (As if having real curriculum staff would have made a difference.) Same unit, coming...

Maybe there's a 'fool me once' aspect to the system, so at least their impact might be limited somehow.

And a last, most sneaky one: When submitting grades, don't use the institution's 'guaranteed grade conversion' if the grading scale is not in the receiving country's standard system (transcript by default, at least two clicks to find and untick it). That's ~6% gap from the mid-market standard. The worst grade reporting services do better than that.

Depending on which framework you're talking about. I care very little about big international boards - they can mandate whatever they want. There are plenty of local/community programs releasing both on paper and digital, who succumbed to nothing, but are alive and well actually. Check local educator forums more often for clues, should you disagree.

Rote testing inherently sacrifices the depth of inquiry gained through project-based learning (PBL). The 'warm feel' of genuine student engagement is lost when every unit must be boiled down to a multiple-choice bubble sheet. High-stakes assessment is necessary, but the constant need for quantifiable metrics inevitably leads to curricular compression.

I've been teaching diverse students my whole life and there were times that I tried to get everything covered by the syllabus, times I tried to go fully project-based, times I've tried to go purely digital. And the pattern I've fallen into now in my late 30s is embracing whatever methodology was popular when I started teaching. I've now got a pretty mixed collection of formal units, informal projects, digital resources, and even a physical portfolio. An added facet of fun for me when I find new content is to decide what the most thematically appropriate format to own it is. For example I own the physical essay rubrics for global citizenship by Magdalena...
